<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439803 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00937: (2026x) Measurements dialog

- page_id: `254439803`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439803/2026x+Measurements+dialog
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:37.633+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Dialogs
- labels: ['measurements-dialog']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Measurements** dialog is designed to display measurements available to be added as columns to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-3 Operational Resource Flow Matrix' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-6 Systems Resource Flow Matrix' space=''], or[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SvcV-6 Services Resource Flow Matrix' space=''] tables.

The **Measurements** dialog consists of two tabs:

- Operational Exchange (or Resource Interaction in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables).
- Operational Exchange Item (or Resource Interaction Item in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables).

The**Operational Exchange** (or **Resource Interaction** in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables) tab displays measurements grouped by the Measurement Sets of the Operational Exchanges (or Resource Interactions in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables). The **Operational Exchange Item** (or**Resource Interaction Item** in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables) tab displays measurements grouped by the Measurement Sets of Operational Exchange Items (or Resource Interaction Items in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables). The measurements displayed in the **Measurements** dialog are collected from the active table.

The purpose of the Measurement Set is grouping the particular measurements. In order to show or hide measurements of a particular Measurement Set, you can expand or collapse the Measurement Set category. For example, Exchange Properties is the grouping Measurement Set of the measurements Accountability, Interoperability Level Achievable, and so forth.

When you select the measurement and click the **OK** button, the selected measurement is added as a new column into the table.

The selected measurement description is displayed in the Description area. If you want to see the description of your custom measurement, you should have specified the documentation when creating a measurement.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To open the **Measurements** dialog

- On the active table toolbar, click Show Columns button and select Measurements . The Measurements dialog opens.

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying Custom Measurements' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The <strong>Operational Exchange Item</strong> (or<strong> Resource Interaction Item</strong> in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables) tab displays measurements grouped by the Measurement Sets of Operational Exchange Items (or Resource Interaction Items in the SV-6 and SvcV-6 tables). The measurements displayed in the <strong>Measurements</strong> dialog are collected from the active table.</p><p>The purpose of the Measurement Set is grouping the particular measurements. In order to show or hide measurements of a particular Measurement Set, you can expand or collapse the Measurement Set category. For example, Exchange Properties is the grouping Measurement Set of the measurements Accountability, Interoperability Level Achievable, and so forth.</p><p>When you select the measurement and click the <strong>OK</strong> button, the selected measurement is added as a new column into the table.</p><p>The selected measurement description is displayed in the Description area. If you want to see the description of your custom measurement, you should have specified the documentation when creating a measurement.</p><p><ac:image ac:alt="Measurements dialog" ac:title="Measurements dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="measurements_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Measurements dialog" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>To open the <strong>Measurements</strong> dialog</p><hr /><ul><li>On the active table toolbar, click <strong>Show Columns</strong> button and select<strong> Measurements</strong>.<br />The <strong>Measurements</strong> dialog opens.</li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="020d71b1-3dd7-4c96-9c20-71993c04d3da" ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying Custom Measurements" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

StV-2 Capability Taxonomy</a></p><h3>Related NAF views</h3><p><br /></p><p><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/UPDM2P/(2026x) (2026x) NCV-2+Capability+Taxonomy">NCV-2 Capability Taxonomy</a></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439534 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00943: (2026x) MODAF All Views Viewpoint

- page_id: `254439534`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439534/2026x+MODAF+All+Views+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:16.515+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Elements that are part of the All View. The All-Views (AVs) provide an overarching description of the architecture, its scope, ownership, timeframe and all of the other meta data that is required in order to effectively search and query architectural models. They also provide a place to record any findings arising from the architecting process. The AVs include a dictionary of the terms used in the construction of the architecture – which helps others fully understand its meaning at a later date. Since the AVs provide critical information for the future access and exploitation of an architectural model their population is essential whenever an architecture is created or modified. The AVs provide a critical input into the processes that provide architectural governance.

The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Elements that are part of the All View. The All-Views (AVs) provide an overarching description of the architecture, its scope, ownership, timeframe and all of the other meta data that is required in order to effectively search and query architectural models. They also provide a place to record any findings arising from the architecting process. The AVs include a dictionary of the terms used in the construction of the architecture – which helps others fully understand its meaning at a later date. Since the AVs provide critical information for the future access and exploitation of an architectural model their population is essential whenever an architecture is created or modified. The AVs provide a critical input into the processes that provide architectural governance.</p><p>The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="0cab72b5-e739-4d1d-a9cb-b3bc1f4acb6c" /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439576 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00944: (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint

- page_id: `254439576`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439576/2026x+MODAF+Operational+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:23.289+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Operational Viewpoint is about real-world activities, the people and machinery that perform them, and the means by which they are performed. The Operational Viewpoint is divided into nine views intended to answer the “who”, “what”, “when”, “where”, “why”, and “how” of a mission.

The Operational Views are common to MODAF and DoDAF; OV-1b, OV-1c, and OV-2 however, have been customized to provide for MOD requirements. The Operational Views describe the tasks and activities, operational elements, and information exchanges required to conduct operations. In MODAF thinking, the OV Views are considered to illustrate the Logical Architecture of the enterprise.

The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The Operational Viewpoint is about real-world activities, the people and machinery that perform them, and the means by which they are performed. The Operational Viewpoint is divided into nine views intended to answer the “who”, “what”, “when”, “where”, “why”, and “how” of a mission.</p><p>The Operational Views are common to MODAF and DoDAF; OV-1b, OV-1c, and OV-2 however, have been customized to provide for MOD requirements. The Operational Views describe the tasks and activities, operational elements, and information exchanges required to conduct operations. In MODAF thinking, the OV Views are considered to illustrate the Logical Architecture of the enterprise.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="51021151-7b24-4f28-ab31-f3d730d0b90f"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jrpPuZ8Vf74" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><br />The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="bd3046ba-6e5c-4b3c-ae1c-08f58ae2ec47" /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439578 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00945: (2026x) MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint

- page_id: `254439578`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439578/2026x+MODAF+Service+Oriented+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:23.557+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Services View (SOV) shows the elements that are part of SOV-1 through SOV-5.

The Service-Orientated View is a description of services needed to directly support the operational domain as described in the Operational View. A service is described as a unit of work through which a particular Resource provides a useful result to a consuming Resource.

The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

A service is described as a unit of work through which a particular Resource provides a useful result to a consuming Resource.</p><p>The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="d78799f7-2af7-4a55-b6b2-d216aa859572" /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439561 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00946: (2026x) MODAF Strategic Viewpoint

- page_id: `254439561`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439561/2026x+MODAF+Strategic+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:20.700+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Strategic View (StV) shows the elements that are part of StV-1 through StV-6. The Strategic Elements are used in the Strategic View that provides an overall Enterprise Architecture assessment of the Capabilities and their relationships facilitating Capability Management (e.g. capability introduction, integration, re-alignment and removal). While an Enterprise will have a number of UPDM Architecture Descriptions that have the Operational, System, Technical Standards, and All Views, only one Strategic View will exist across a number of Architecture Descriptions.

The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The Strategic View (StV) shows the elements that are part of StV-1 through StV-6. The Strategic Elements are used in the Strategic View that provides an overall Enterprise Architecture assessment of the Capabilities and their relationships facilitating Capability Management (e.g. capability introduction, integration, re-alignment and removal). While an Enterprise will have a number of UPDM Architecture Descriptions that have the Operational, System, Technical Standards, and All Views, only one Strategic View will exist across a number of Architecture  Descriptions.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fa507d40-6514-46a8-856e-fb8b6a3a2dd0"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xD91EYVU-bM" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro> <ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b1e62cfd-c6c8-4fe1-98e3-466160bb9013"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LB1Ure3ravQ" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p>The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="71f190eb-5b5e-47a3-b706-3d96d45eeff2" /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439580 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00947: (2026x) MODAF Systems Viewpoint

- page_id: `254439580`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439580/2026x+MODAF+Systems+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:23.870+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The System View (SV) shows the elements that are part of SV-1 through SV-12. Models in the System Viewpoint represent alternate realizations in terms of equipment capability of the operational capabilities expressed through models in the Operational Viewpoint and in the User Requirements. The System Viewpoint primarily addresses the specification of the system capability needed (rather than implementation details). Significant changes originally made in MODAF improved the ability for modelers to represent configuration of capability that include people as well as systems and platforms.

The views of this view are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The System View (SV) shows the elements that are part of SV-1 through SV-12. Models in the System Viewpoint represent alternate realizations in terms of equipment capability of the operational capabilities expressed through models in the Operational Viewpoint and in the User Requirements. The System Viewpoint primarily addresses the specification of the system capability needed (rather than implementation details). Significant changes originally made in MODAF improved the ability for modelers to represent configuration of capability that include people as well as systems and platforms.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7f36730a-3425-4657-b9c9-b69e909746b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qvL33ckmJJY" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p>The views of this view are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="27663c43-db4d-4fc0-bf8e-02615a02e2aa" /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439582 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00948: (2026x) MODAF Technical Standards Viewpoint

- page_id: `254439582`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439582/2026x+MODAF+Technical+Standards+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:24.070+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Technical View (TV) shows the elements that are part of TV-1 through TV-2.

The Technical View is a set of views delineating standards, rules, notations, and conventions that apply to the implementation of the system architecture. When the standards profile is tied to the system elements to which they apply, TV-1 serves as the bridge between the SV and TV. SV-9 forecasts relate to the TV-1 in that a timed technology forecast may contribute to the decision to retire or phase out the use of a certain standard in connection with a system element. Similarly, SV-9 forecasts relate to TV-2 standards forecasts in that a certain standard may be adopted depending on a certain technology becoming available (e.g., the availability of Java Script may influence the decision to adopt a new HTML standard).

MODAF extends the core DoDAF Technical Standards Views to include non-technical standards and policies applicable to the architecture such as operational doctrine, industry process standards, etc. Additionally, the TV-1 may also document policies and standards applicable to the operational or business context. MODAF also distinguishes between ‘applicability’ and ‘conformance’ with regard to architectural elements. If a standard is applicable to a given architecture, that architecture need not be fully conformant with the standard. The degree of conformance to a given standard may be judged on a risk basis at an approval point. An association between a Standard and an architectural element is not to be interpreted as stating the level of compliance of the element is fully compliant with that Standard. Additional evidences would need to be given (outside MODAF) to confirm the level of compliance. Finally, MODAF adds the explicit requirement that any Standards cited in TV-1 View must, where appropriate, be in accordance with the trend towards open architectures – i.e. standards which encourage stove-piped systems are expressly prohibited.

The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The Technical View (TV) shows the elements that are part of TV-1 through TV-2. </p><p>The Technical View is a set of views delineating standards, rules, notations, and conventions that apply to the implementation of the system architecture. When the standards profile is tied to the system elements to which they apply, TV-1 serves as the bridge between the SV and TV. SV-9 forecasts relate to the TV-1 in that a timed technology forecast may contribute to the decision to retire or phase out the use of a certain standard in connection with a system element. Similarly, SV-9 forecasts relate to TV-2 standards forecasts in that a certain standard may be adopted depending on a certain technology becoming available (e.g., the availability of Java Script may influence the decision to adopt a new HTML standard). </p><p>MODAF extends the core DoDAF Technical Standards Views to include non-technical standards and policies applicable to the architecture such as operational doctrine, industry process standards, etc. Additionally, the TV-1 may also document policies and standards applicable to the operational or business context. MODAF also distinguishes between ‘applicability’ and ‘conformance’ with regard to architectural elements. If a standard is applicable to a given architecture, that architecture need not be fully conformant with the standard. The degree of conformance to a given standard may be judged on a risk basis at an approval point. An association between a Standard and an architectural element is not to be interpreted as stating the level of compliance of the element is fully compliant with that Standard. Additional evidences would need to be given (outside MODAF) to confirm the level of compliance. Finally, MODAF adds the explicit requirement that any Standards cited in TV-1 View must, where appropriate, be in accordance with the trend towards open architectures – i.e. standards which encourage stove-piped systems are expressly prohibited.</p><p>The views of this viewpoint are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="77a7ee4f-cf13-45cc-8593-ecdc77324b42" /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439641 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00949: (2026x) MODAF. AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters

- page_id: `254439641`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439641/2026x+MODAF.+AcV-1+Acquisition+Clusters
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:27.330+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Acquisition Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Acquisition Clusters (AcV-1) View describes how acquisition projects are grouped in organisational terms as a coherent portfolio of acquisition programmes.

The AcV-1 View provides a way of describing the organizational relationships between multiple acquisition projects, each of which is responsible for delivering individual systems or capabilities. By definition, this View covers acquisition programmes consisting of multiple projects and will generally not be developed by those building Architectures for an individual project. In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.

The AcV-1 View is hierarchical in nature. Higher level groupings of projects (or, rather the organisations that own these projects) form acquisition clusters.

The intent of an AcV-1 View Product is to show:

- All of the acquisition projects delivering systems or system of systems (SoS) within the acquisition programmes under consideration.
- Other systems and SoS which may have a bearing on the Architecture.
- How the systems will be best integrated into acquisition clusters.
- The nesting of acquisition clusters to form a hierarchy.

**Implementation**

AcV-1 can be represented using:

- An AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- An AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix which is an editable Dependency Matrix.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix

**Related views**

In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Actual Organization
- Actual Post
- Actual Project
- Organizational Project Relationship
- Project Ownership
- Project Sequence
- Project Type
- Project Milestone
- Project Milestone Role
- Actual Project Milestone Role
- Status Indicators

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating AcV-1 diagram
- Building AcV-1 matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

By definition, this View covers acquisition programmes consisting of multiple projects and will generally not be developed by those building Architectures for an individual project. In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.</p><p>The AcV-1 View is hierarchical in nature. Higher level groupings of projects (or, rather the organisations that own these projects) form acquisition clusters.</p><p>The intent of an AcV-1 View Product is to show:</p><ul><li>All of the acquisition projects delivering systems or system of systems (SoS) within the acquisition programmes under consideration.</li><li>Other systems and SoS which may have a bearing on the Architecture.</li><li>How the systems will be best integrated into acquisition clusters.</li><li>The nesting of acquisition clusters to form a hierarchy.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>AcV-1 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>An AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix which is an editable Dependency Matrix.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Acv_1.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="59a0d9cf-2051-420c-8eb3-9e4cc9a3b06f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Actual Organization</li><li>Actual Post</li><li>Actual Project</li><li>Organizational Project Relationship</li><li>Project Ownership</li><li>Project Sequence</li><li>Project Type</li><li>Project Milestone</li><li>Project Milestone Role</li><li>Actual Project Milestone Role</li><li>Status Indicators</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="59dbb89e-beac-4dc3-b8bc-db7e93b5088a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating AcV-1 diagram</li><li>Building AcV-1 matrix</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

## PAGE 00950: (2026x) MODAF. AcV-2 Programme Timelines

- page_id: `254439634`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439634/2026x+MODAF.+AcV-2+Programme+Timelines
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:26.963+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Acquisition Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The AcV-2 view provides a timeline perspective on programmes.

The AcV-2 View is intended primarily to support the acquisition and fielding processes including the management of dependencies between projects and the integration of all the DLODs to achieve a successfully integrated military capability.

For capability-based procurement, these work streams might conveniently be equated with Defence Lines of Development (DLODs).

**Implementation**

AcV-2 can be represented using an AcV-2 diagram which is realized as a Gantt Chart.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Acv-2 Programme Timelines

**Related views**

The use of AcV-2 should support the management of capability delivery and be aligned with the capability phasing view (StV-3), if one exists.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Actual Project
- Project Status
- Project Sequence
- Project Theme
- Actual Project Milestone
- Increment Milestone
- Out Of Service Milestone
- Milestone Sequence
- Project Milestone

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating AcV-2 diagram

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Project Status Application Wizard

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439595 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00951: (2026x) MODAF. AV-1 Overview and Summary Information

- page_id: `254439595`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439595/2026x+MODAF.+AV-1+Overview+and+Summary+Information
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:24.801+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF All Views Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The overview and summary information contained within the AV-1 product provides an executive-level summary information in a consistent form that allows for quick reference and comparison between architectural descriptions. AV-1 includes assumptions, constraints, and limitations that may affect high-level decisions relating to an architecture. In an enterprise repository environment, individual architectures are mapped against enterprise phases to provide context between the architectures. AV-1 is usually a structured text product. Organizations may create a template for the AV-1 that can then be used to create a consistent set of information across different architecture-based projects.

**Implementation**

AV-1 can be represented:

- Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data. Since only a partial AV-1 report can be generated, the rest of data must be filled in manually.
- Using a AV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of AV-1 Overview and Summary Information report

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Architectural Description
- Defines Architecture
- Architecture Metadata

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Managing Reports' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439616 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00952: (2026x) MODAF. AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

- page_id: `254439616`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439616/2026x+MODAF.+AV-2+Integrated+Dictionary
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:25.988+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF All Views Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

An AV-2 presents all the Elements used in an architecture as a standalone structure. An AV-2 presents all the Elements as a specialization hierarchy, provides a text definition for each one and references the source of the element. An AV-2 shows elements from the Ontology that have been used in the architecture and new elements that have been introduced by the architecture. Architectures often introduce new terms – usually because the architecture is covering new technology or business processes. The purpose of the AV-2 is to provide a local extension of the Ontology to explain the terms and abbreviations used in building the architecture.

**Implementation**

AV-2 can be represented:

- Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data.
- An AV-2 table. If you have AV-2 diagrams from previous versions, they will be migrated into the AV-1 diagram when loading the project with UPDM 17.0.2.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of AV-2 Integrated Dictionary report

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Definition
- Alias
- Same As

#### PANEL: Related procedure

Related procedure

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Managing Reports' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439656 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00953: (2026x) MODAF. OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

- page_id: `254439656`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439656/2026x+MODAF.+OV-1+High-Level+Operational+Concept+Graphic
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:27.872+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of High-level Operational Concept Graphic is to provide a high-level graphical and textual description of operational concept (high level organizations, missions, geographic configuration, connectivity, etc) of what the architecture is supposed to do, and how it is supposed to do it. The OV-1, along with the corresponding AV-1 product is intended to serve as an executive summary of the architecture.

**Implementation**

OV-1 can be represented using:

- A link to an external document.
- An OV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- An OV-1 Free Form diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure Diagram.

**Sample** 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- High-Level Operational Concept
- Concept Role
- Arbitrary Relationship

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-1 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439648 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00954: (2026x) MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

- page_id: `254439648`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439648/2026x+MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Internal+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:27.540+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.
- A SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Known Resource
- Needline
- Node Port
- Node Role
- Operational Exchange
- Service Channel
- Service
- Property
- Request

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-2 diagram
- Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.</p><p>Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.</p><p>The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.</p><p>The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="62a36051-3a08-4516-b192-e507c7f0838b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Known Resource</li><li>Needline</li><li>Node Port</li><li>Node Role</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Service Channel</li><li>Service</li><li>Property</li><li>Request</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f593d17f-1dd2-4d43-bd63-00047de445a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-2 diagram</li><li>Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439693 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00955: (2026x) MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

- page_id: `254439693`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439693/2026x+MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:30.239+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Node Connectivity Description is intended to track the need to exchange information from specific operational nodes (that play a key role in the architecture) to others. An OV-2 does not depict the connectivity between the nodes. MODAF modifies the OV-2 in two ways. First it recommends that an OV-2 diagram (now OV2a) shows the platforms or geographic locations at which operational nodes are deployed. Secondly it provides additional information (OV-2b) about each needline in the form of a requirements specification. There are now four types of needlines identified as follows:

1. InformationExchange
2. EnergyFlow
3. MaterielFlow
4. MovementOfPeople

In addition, MODAF permits service-oriented architectures. Instead of needlines between nodes, it is possible simply to show which services the nodes provide and consume. Finally, MODAF again permits known resources to be shown in an OV-2. However, this must be clearly shown as a KnownResource in an OV-2 model. LogicalArchitecture, which is the container class for all the nodes and KnownResources, is introduced.

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.
- A SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Node
- Logical Architecture
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element
- Node Port
- Node Role
- Needline
- Capability
- Exhibits
- Actual Location
- Location Type
- Energy
- Materiel
- Is Capable Of Performing
- Operational Activity
- Known Resource
- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post
- Problem Domain
- Security Domain

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-2 diagram
- Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Operational Exchange Creation Wizard
- Operational Exchange Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Operational Node Connectivity Description is intended to track the need to exchange information from specific operational nodes (that play a key role in the architecture) to others. An OV-2 does not depict the connectivity between the nodes. MODAF modifies the OV-2 in two ways. First it recommends that an OV-2 diagram (now OV2a) shows the platforms or geographic locations at which operational nodes are deployed. Secondly it provides additional information (OV-2b) about each needline in the form of a requirements specification. There are now four types of needlines identified as follows:</p><ol><li>InformationExchange</li><li>EnergyFlow</li><li>MaterielFlow</li><li>MovementOfPeople</li></ol><p>In addition, MODAF permits service-oriented architectures. Instead of needlines between nodes, it is possible simply to show which services the nodes provide and consume. Finally, MODAF again permits known resources to be shown in an OV-2. However, this must be clearly shown as a KnownResource in an OV-2 model. LogicalArchitecture, which is the container class for all the nodes and KnownResources, is introduced.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-2 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>An OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li><li>A UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>A SysML Block Definition diagram.</li><li>A SysML Internal Block diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_2.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities. </p><p>Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.</p><p>The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.</p><p>The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f33505b5-64f9-4313-b17d-5dc11c676a93"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Node</li><li>Logical Architecture</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Exchange Element</li><li>Node Port</li><li>Node Role</li><li>Needline</li><li>Capability</li><li>Exhibits</li><li>Actual Location</li><li>Location Type</li><li>Energy</li><li>Materiel</li><li>Is Capable Of Performing</li><li>Operational Activity</li><li>Known Resource</li><li>Resource Artifact</li><li>Software</li><li>Capability Configuration</li><li>Organization</li><li>Post</li><li>Problem Domain</li><li>Security Domain</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6ff755d-588d-438c-839d-4affdf254603"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-2 diagram</li><li>Creating Operational Exchanges in OV-2 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f46a0ac0-094b-4621-9b5d-8f4a1ff04bc8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related references</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Exchange Creation Wizard</li><li>Operational Exchange Manager Dialog</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439629 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00956: (2026x) MODAF. OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

- page_id: `254439629`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439629/2026x+MODAF.+OV-3+Operational+Information+Exchange+Matrix
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:26.737+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

Information exchanges express the relationship across the three basic architecture data elements of an OV (operational activities, operational nodes, and information flow) with a focus on the specific aspects of the information flow and the information content.

The Information Exchanges of the OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes shown in the OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes).

**Implementation**

OV-3 can be represented using:

- An OV-3 table.
- An OV-3 role-based table. A role-based table represents exchanges between Node Roles (node usages).
- An OV-3 spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

**Related views**

An OV-3 is initially constructed from the information contained in the Operational Node Connectivity Description (OV-2).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Node
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element
- Energy
- Materiel
- Resource Artifact
- Post
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Operational Activity

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-3 table
- Displaying Custom Measurements

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>Information exchanges express the relationship across the three basic architecture data elements of an OV (operational activities, operational nodes, and information flow) with a focus on the specific aspects of the information flow and the information content.</p><p>The Information Exchanges of the OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes shown in the OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes).</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-3 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-3 table.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">An OV-3 role-based table.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95fc2252-c5b7-4d99-813f-69e414365fe0"><ac:rich-text-body><p>A role-based table represents exchanges between Node Roles (node usages).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>An OV-3 spreadsheet report.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_3.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439624 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00957: (2026x) MODAF. OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

- page_id: `254439624`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439624/2026x+MODAF.+OV-4+Organizational+Relationships+Chart
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:26.559+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Organizational Relationships Chart illustrates the command structure or relationships (as opposed to relationships with respect to a business process flow) among human roles, organizations, or organization types that are the key players in architecture. MODAF divides The OV-4 in two views: an OV-4 Typical and an OV-4 Actual. The former is exactly as the DoDAF OV-4, while the latter is a special form of the SV-1; where the resources are restricted to being organizational.

**Implementation**

OV-4 can be represented using:

- An OV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

**Related views**

The organizations that are modeled using an OV-4 in the Operational Viewpoint may also appear in other views, for example, SV-1 (organizational constituents of a capability configuration), AcV-1 (actual organizations that own projects), and SV-5 (organizational resources performs functions).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Organization
- Post
- Person
- Command
- Competence
- Provides Competence
- Requires Competence
- Actual Organization
- Actual Post
- Actual Person
- Fills Post
- Actual Organization Relationship
- Actual Organization Role
- Operational Activity
- Owns Process

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-4 diagram
- Instantiating Structures

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

MODAF divides The OV-4 in two views: an OV-4 Typical and an OV-4 Actual. The former is exactly as the DoDAF OV-4, while the latter is a special form of the SV-1; where the resources are restricted to being organizational.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-4 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li><li>A SysML Block Definition diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_4.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439748 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00958: (2026x) MODAF. OV-5 Operational Activity Model

- page_id: `254439748`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439748/2026x+MODAF.+OV-5+Operational+Activity+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:33.412+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Activity Model describes the operations that are normally conducted in the course of achieving a mission or a business goal, from a net-centric perspective. It describes capabilities, operational activities (or tasks), input and output (I/O) flows between activities, and I/O flows to/from activities that are outside the scope of the architecture. It is imperative that the levels-of-detail between the OV-2, OV-3, and OV-5 remain cohesive. For example, if one diagram of OV-2 operational nodes is developed that shows aggregated organizations only, then it is imperative that the corresponding OV-5 product be developed to show only those operational activities that are meaningful with respect to these operational nodes. Similarly, the information exchanges of OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes depicted in OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes). The net-centric OV-5 may be used in the following ways:

- Delineate lines of dependency on external activities when coupled with an OV-2.
- Highlight information flows to depict the status of the information's refinement (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Provide the critical foundation for depicting Task, Post, Process, and Use (TPPU) activity sequencing and timing in the OV-6a, OV-6b, and OV-6c.
- Identify critical mission threads and operational information exchanges by annotating which activities are critical, i.e., identify the activities in the model that are critical.

**Implementation**

OV-5 can be represented using:

- An OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity hierarchies. This diagram is based on the UML Class diagram.
- An OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Activity diagram.
- A SysML Block diagram.
- A SysML Activity diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-5 Operational Activity hierarchy Model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of OV-5 Operational Activity Model

**Related views**

The OV-5 and OV-2 are, to a degree, complements of each other. An OV-5 focuses on the operational activities whereas OV-2 focuses on the operational nodes. Due to the relationship between nodes and operational activities, these types of view should normally be developed together.

To maintain this independence from implementation, the logical Nodes in an OV-2 are used to represent the structure which carries out the Operational Activities. Operational Activities are realized as Functions (SV-4) which are the “how” to the Operational Activities’ “what”, for example, they are specified in terms of the resources that carry them out.

The activities described in an OV-5 may be Standard Operational Activities which are defined in the StV-6 (which also maps the activities to corresponding capabilities).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Activity
- Operational Parameter
- Standard Operational Activity
- Node
- Is Capable Of Performing
- Operational Activity Action
- Operational Activity Edge
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element
- Energy
- Materiel

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Model diagram
- Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Flow Model diagram
- Displaying possible Operational Exchanges on the selected Operational Activity Edge

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Operational Exchange Creation Wizard
- Operational Exchange Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It describes capabilities, operational activities (or tasks), input and output (I/O) flows between activities, and I/O flows to/from activities that are outside the scope of the architecture. The net-centric OV-5 may be used in the following ways:</p><ul><li>Delineate lines of dependency on external activities when coupled with an OV-2.</li><li>Highlight information flows to depict the status of the information's refinement (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Provide the critical foundation for depicting Task, Post, Process, and Use (TPPU) activity sequencing and timing in the OV-6a, OV-6b, and OV-6c.</li><li>Identify critical mission threads and operational information exchanges by annotating which activities are critical, i.e., identify the activities in the model that are critical.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-5 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity hierarchies. This diagram is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>An OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li><li>A UML Activity diagram.</li><li>A SysML Block diagram.</li><li>A SysML Activity diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_5.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. OV-5 Operational Activity Model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Fragment of OV-5 Operational Activity Model</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The OV-5 and OV-2 are, to a degree, complements of each other. Operational Activities are realized as Functions (SV-4) which are the “how” to the Operational Activities’ “what”, for example, they are specified in terms of the resources that carry them out.</p><p>The activities described in an OV-5 may be Standard Operational Activities which are defined in the StV-6 (which also maps the activities to corresponding capabilities).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="297e311a-a71b-4025-8383-99f459a353d3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Activity</li><li>Operational Parameter</li><li>Standard Operational Activity</li><li>Node</li><li>Is Capable Of Performing</li><li>Operational Activity Action</li><li>Operational Activity Edge</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Exchange Element</li><li>Energy</li><li>Materiel</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3bfa42f4-f543-470e-aa1f-786f99cdedf9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Model diagram</li><li>Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Flow Model diagram</li><li>Displaying possible Operational Exchanges on the selected Operational Activity Edge</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="28d6248e-d5e0-43e2-beb4-b99cb2d5bd6c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related references</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Exchange Creation Wizard</li><li>Operational Exchange Manager Dialog</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439743 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00959: (2026x) MODAF. OV-6a Operational Rules Model

- page_id: `254439743`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439743/2026x+MODAF.+OV-6a+Operational+Rules+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:33.166+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Rules Model specifies operational or business rules that are constraints on an enterprise, a mission, operation, business, or an architecture. While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At the mission level, an OV-6a may consist of doctrine, guidance, rules of engagement, and so forth. At the operation level, rules may include such things as a military Operational Plan (OPLAN). At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed 
rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.

**Implementation**

OV-6a can be represented using:

- An OV-6a table.
- An OV-6a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6a Operational Rules Model

**Related views**

An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.

As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Constraint
- Node
- Operational Activity
- Entity Item
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-6a table

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At the mission level, an OV-6a may consist of doctrine, guidance, rules of engagement, and so forth. At the operation level, rules may include such things as a military Operational Plan (OPLAN). At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed<br />rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6a can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-6a table.</li><li>An OV-6a spreadsheet report.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6a.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. OV-6a Operational Rules Model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6a Operational Rules Model</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.</p><p>As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58c52e31-42e9-409b-bab5-8ad8152b9b6d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Constraint</li><li>Node</li><li>Operational Activity</li><li>Entity Item</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Exchange Element</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f51a094-1bff-4ab7-a695-e2941c994888"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-6a table</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439759 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00960: (2026x) MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

- page_id: `254439759`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439759/2026x+MODAF.+OV-6b+Operational+State+Transition+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:34.460+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational State Transition Description is a graphical method of describing how an operational node or activity responds to various events by changing its state. The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.

Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.

**Implementation**

OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

**Related views**

An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational State Description
- Operational State
- Node

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Operational State Transition Description is a graphical method of describing how an operational node or activity responds to various events by changing its state. The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.</p><p>Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6b.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6b Operational State Transition Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ba6fc0a-d952-4e0d-bf5c-28efe945ca02"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational State Description</li><li>Operational State</li><li>Node</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439754 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00961: (2026x) MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `254439754`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439754/2026x+MODAF.+OV-6c+Operational+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:33.710+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Event-Trace Description provides a time-ordered examination of the information exchanges between participating operational nodes as a result of a particular operational thread or scenario. Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.

The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following:

- Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.
- Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.
- Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.

**Implementation**

OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

**Related views**

The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.

The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Event Trace
- Operational Message
- Operational Exchange
- Node
- Node Role

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Operational Exchange Creation Wizard
- Operational Exchange Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.</p><p>The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following: </p><ul><li>Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.</li><li>Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.</li><li>Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6c.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.</p><p>The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a1248623-bce8-4ee0-b57e-8403cecfccf3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Event Trace</li><li>Operational Message</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Node</li><li>Node Role</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fb6c875b-ab23-4910-af35-a5507301b02a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related references</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Exchange Creation Wizard</li><li>Operational Exchange Manager Dialog</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254439725 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00962: (2026x) MODAF. OV-7 Information Model

- page_id: `254439725`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/254439725/2026x+MODAF.+OV-7+Information+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:07:32.023+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Logical Data Model describes the structure of an architecture domain’s system data types and the structural business process rules (defined in the architecture’s Operational View) that govern the system data. It provides a definition of architecture domain data types, their attributes or characteristics, and their interrelationships. An OV7, including the domain’s system data types or entity definitions, is a key element in supporting interoperability between architectures, since these definitions may be used by other organizations to determine system data compatibility. Often, different organizations may use the same entity name to mean very different kinds of system data with different internal structure. This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data 
models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.

In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.

****

**Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration**

You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.

Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.

**Implementation**

OV-7 can be represented using:

- An OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-7 Information Model

**Related views**

An Operational Information Entity within an OV-7 may be an Information Element in an OV-3 or an Activity Flow Object in an OV-5.

Note that MODAF talks about ‘information’ in the Operational Viewpoint and ‘data’ in the System Viewpoint. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Logical Data Model
- Entity Item
- Entity Attribute
- Entity Relationship
- Details
- Exchange Element

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-7 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
